questions.optionB: Infectious mononucleosis, Burkitt lymphoma, nasopharyngeal carcinoma, Hodgkin lymphoma, and post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder
questions.explanation: EBV (HHV-4): DNA virus, herpesvirus family. Infects B cells (via CD21 receptor). Causes: infectious mononucleosis (heterophile antibody positive — Monospot test, atypical lymphocytes on smear, pharyngitis, lymphadenopathy, splenomegaly). Malignancies: Burkitt lymphoma (African endemic, t(8;14) c-MYC), nasopharyngeal carcinoma, Hodgkin lymphoma (mixed cellularity), PTLD, primary CNS lymphoma (HIV), oral hairy leukoplakia (HIV). Latent infection in B cells. Reference: Ananthanarayan — EBV.

questions.questionText: Hepatitis B virus (HBV) is unique among hepatitis viruses because it:
questions.optionB: Is a DNA virus (partially double-stranded), uses reverse transcriptase, and can integrate into host genome → hepatocellular carcinoma
questions.explanation: HBV: Hepadnaviridae, partially dsDNA (relaxed circular DNA), smallest DNA virus genome. Unique: uses reverse transcriptase (DNA → RNA → DNA) despite being a DNA virus. Can integrate into host genome → HCC (even without cirrhosis). Transmission: blood, sexual, vertical (mother-to-child). Serology: HBsAg (infection marker), anti-HBs (immunity), HBeAg (high infectivity), anti-HBc IgM (acute), anti-HBc IgG (past/chronic). Vaccine: recombinant HBsAg (universal immunization). Treatment: tenofovir, entecavir, PEG-IFN. Reference: Ananthanarayan — HBV.

questions.optionB: Cervical carcinoma (E6 degrades p53, E7 inactivates Rb), oropharyngeal cancer, anal cancer
questions.explanation: HPV: non-enveloped dsDNA virus, infects squamous epithelium. High-risk types (16, 18, 31, 33): cervical cancer (70% caused by 16 and 18), anal, penile, oropharyngeal cancers. Oncoproteins: E6 (degrades p53 via ubiquitin ligase), E7 (binds and inactivates Rb → releases E2F). Low-risk types (6, 11): genital warts (condylomata acuminata), laryngeal papillomatosis. Koilocytes: characteristic HPV cytological finding (perinuclear clearing). Vaccine: Gardasil 9 (9-valent). Pap smear: screening. Reference: Ananthanarayan — HPV.

questions.questionText: Herpes simplex virus (HSV) establishes latency in:
questions.optionB: Sensory ganglia (trigeminal ganglion for HSV-1, sacral ganglia for HSV-2)
questions.explanation: HSV: establishes lifelong latency in sensory nerve ganglia. HSV-1: primarily orofacial (cold sores, gingivostomatitis, herpes keratitis, encephalitis — temporal lobe) → latent in TRIGEMINAL ganglion. HSV-2: primarily genital herpes → latent in SACRAL (S2-S3) ganglia. Reactivation: stress, immunosuppression, UV exposure, fever. Diagnosis: Tzanck smear (multinucleated giant cells — also seen in VZV), PCR, viral culture. Treatment: acyclovir. Neonatal herpes (HSV-2): acquired during delivery, high mortality. Reference: Ananthanarayan — HSV.

questions.questionText: CMV (Cytomegalovirus/HHV-5) in immunocompromised patients commonly causes:
questions.optionB: Retinitis (pizza-pie appearance), colitis, esophagitis, pneumonitis, and characteristic 'owl-eye' nuclear inclusions
questions.explanation: CMV (HHV-5): largest herpesvirus. In immunocompetent: usually asymptomatic or mono-like syndrome (heterophile-negative mononucleosis). In immunocompromised (HIV CD4<50, transplant): CMV retinitis (most common — 'pizza-pie' / cottage cheese and ketchup fundus), CMV colitis (bloody diarrhea), CMV esophagitis, CMV pneumonitis (BMT). Congenital CMV: most common congenital infection (sensorineural hearing loss — #1 cause, periventricular calcifications, hepatosplenomegaly). Histology: large cells with intranuclear 'owl-eye' inclusions. Treatment: ganciclovir, valganciclovir. Reference: Ananthanarayan — CMV.
